concepts.title: Level 1: Terminal Anatomy & The Shell Prompt

content.why_exists: Before you can control a system, you must understand the bridge between you and the operating system. The terminal is your cockpit.
content.problem: You sit in front of a computer and want to talk to the kernel, but you don't have a mouse or a GUI. How do you issue commands and see results?
content.beginner_explanation: The terminal (or TTY) is just a window that displays text. The 'Shell' (like Bash) is the program running inside that window that reads your typing, translates it for the Kernel, and shows you the result.
content.visual_story: Think of the shell as a translator. You speak 'User', the kernel speaks 'Machine Code'. The Shell translates your command 'ls' into 'System Call: list files'.
step_by_step: Type your first command 'whoami' and press Enter.
content.common_mistakes: Confusing the 'Terminal' (the window) with the 'Shell' (the logic engine). Also, typing commands in the wrong case—Linux is case-sensitive!
content.debugging_thinking: If you type a command and see 'command not found', your first check should be: 'Did I spell it right?' and 'Is it in my PATH?'
content.real_workflow: Sysadmins often spend 99% of their time in a terminal because it is faster, automatable, and works over slow network connections (SSH).
content.kernel_internals: The terminal interacts with the kernel through the PTY (Pseudo-terminal) subsystem. When you press Enter, the TTY driver sends an interrupt to the kernel, which then wakes up the shell process to read the buffer.
mini_tasks: Find the name of your computer using 'uname -n'.

commands.explanation: Asks the shell: 'Who is the effective user logged into this session?'
Prints detailed information about the kernel version and system architecture.
Asks the environment: 'Which shell program am I currently running?'

content.why_exists: A computer is a collection of data. Navigation is how you locate and access that data efficiently.
content.problem: You need to find a configuration file in '/etc', but you are currently in your home directory. How do you move across the system without a file explorer?
content.beginner_explanation: Linux uses a single unified tree. Everything starts at '/' (the root). You 'move' around this tree using 'cd' (change directory).
content.visual_story: Imagine a giant tree with branches. '/' is the trunk. '/home' is a major branch. '/home/user' is a smaller branch. You are like a squirrel jumping from branch to branch.
step_by_step: Type 'pwd' to see exactly where you are sitting right now.
Type 'ls' to see what branches (folders) or leaves (files) are reachable from here.
Type 'cd /etc' to jump to the system configuration branch.
Type 'cd ~' to jump back to your personal nest (home).
content.common_mistakes: Forgetting the leading '/' for absolute paths. If you type 'cd etc' while in '/home', it will fail because there is no 'etc' folder inside 'home'.
content.debugging_thinking: If 'cd' fails, run 'ls -d path' to verify the target actually exists and is a directory.
content.real_workflow: Engineers use 'cd -' to quickly toggle back and forth between two directories, like a 'previous channel' button on a remote.
content.kernel_internals: Every process in Linux has a 'Current Working Directory' (CWD) attribute in its Task Descriptor. The 'cd' command is a shell builtin because it must modify the shell's own CWD, not a child's.

mini_labs.task: Navigate to /tmp and create a folder.

commands.explanation: Print Working Directory. Tells you your absolute location in the VFS.
Jump to the absolute root of the entire system.
Lists files and appends a '/' to directories so you can tell them apart visually.

content.why_exists: Security and configuration. You don't want your private keys or system configs visible to everyone by default.
content.problem: You want to hide a configuration file from normal 'ls' output, and you want to ensure only YOU can read it.
content.beginner_explanation: In Linux, starting a filename with a '.' makes it 'hidden'. Permissions (Read, Write, Execute) decide who can touch what.
content.visual_story: Think of a file like a locked box. The owner has the key. The group has a spare. The 'others' (everyone else) have to look through the window or stay out.
content.common_mistakes: Setting 'chmod 777' on everything to fix errors. This is a massive security hole and a 'lazy engineer' red flag.
content.debugging_thinking: See 'Permission Denied'? Immediately check 'ls -l' and see if you are the owner or part of the group.
content.real_workflow: Deployment scripts use 'chmod +x' to make scripts runnable, but maintain '600' on SSH keys so other users can't steal them.
content.kernel_internals: Permissions are stored in the file's Inode. When you try to 'open()', the kernel checks your UID/GID against the Inode's mode bits before granting access.
interview_questions: What is the difference between 755 and 644 permissions?
How do you find all files owned by 'root' in /home?

commands.explanation: Long listing including hidden files. Shows the full metadata (Inode state).
Set permissions so ONLY the owner can read/write. Everyone else is blocked.
Displays the complete Inode data, including timestamps and block counts.
